coredata.dc:title: Microwave synthesis of histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dots/Ni-Co LDH with flower ball structure for supercapacitor
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Qiu, Hengrui
Sun, Xuejiao
An, Shengli
Lan, Dawei
Cui, Jinlong
Zhang, Yongqiang
He, Wenxiu
coredata.dc:description: A flower ball-like histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dots/Ni-Co LDH (His-GQD/LDH) composite is synthesized via microwave method. The GQDs are uniformly interspersed on LDH surface and the radius of flower balls is approximately 200 nm. The synergistic effect of His-GQD and LDH can signally increase the specific surface areas and conductivity of the composite, thus endowing the composite high specific capacitance (1526 F g−1) and an admirable cycling stability (82.36% capacitance retention). Moreover, we have manufactured a supercapacitor employing His-GQD/LDH and active carbon (AC) as positive/negative electrodes. The device exhibits the maximum energy of 48.89 W h kg−1 at 0.80 kW kg−1, as well as prominent cycling stability (91.13% capacitance retention). This work provides a practicle experimental method of synthesizing hybridizing histidine-functionalized carbon derivatives with LDH by microwave synthesis. Meanwhile, we are optimistic to believe that the electrode material can be extensively applied for supercapacitor because of its splendid electrochemical properties and facile synthesis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: His-GQD/LDH
Flower ball
Microwave method
Supercapacitors

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Energy Conversion and Storage and Environmental Technologies

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Inner Mongolia
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of (a) His-GQD/LDH and (b) other materials.
XPS spectra of His-GQD/LDH: (a) C 1s, (b) Co 2p, (c) N 1s and (d) Ni 2p.
(a) BET and (b) BJH curves of samples.
SEM images of (a and b) Ni(OH)2, (c and d) Co(OH)2, (e and f) LDH, and (g and h) His-GQD/LDH.
TEM (a and b), HRTEM (c) and elemental mapping (d) images of His-GQD/LDH (Inset:SAED pattern).

Cyclic voltammogram of (a) His-GQD/LDH and control samples at 40 mV s−1, (b) His-GQD/LDH at 5–40 mV s−1. (c) b values of His-GQD/LDH, LDH, Ni(OH)2, Co(OH)2 and (d) capacitance control ratios of His-GQD/LDH at 5–40 mV s−1.

GCD curves of (a) His-GQD/LDH and control samples at 1 A g−1, (b) His-GQD/LDH at 1–10 A g−1. The (c) rate performances and (d) cycling performances of His-GQD/LDH, LDH, Ni(OH)2 and Co(OH)2 electrodes.
Illustration of the (a) appearance and (b) composition of supercapacitor. CV curves of (c) the His-GQD/LDH and AC electrodes at 40 mV s−1 and (d) the device at 1.3–1.8 V.
The performances of supercapacitors: (a) CV, (b) GCD, (c) Ragone plot and (d) Cycling property. Inset: (c) light up LED and (d) the GCD curves of first/final cycle.

head.ce:title: Microwave synthesis of histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dots/Ni-Co LDH with flower ball structure for supercapacitor

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Inner Mongolia University of Science & Technology, Baotou, Inner Mongolia 014010, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Inner Mongolia University of Science & Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Inner Mongolia University of Science & Technology, Baotou, Inner Mongolia 014010, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of synthesis procedures of His-GQD/LDH electrode and asymmetric supercapacitor is assembled using His-GQD/LDH, active carbon electrodes.

His-GQD is obtained by pyrolysis reaction. After histidine functionalization, His-GQD carries abundant groups and can effectively link LDH. At the same time, N-doping also plays an important role in improving conductivity and making electron transport more rapidly. Notably, the histidine as nitrogen source is very environmentally friendly. Therefore, we use His-GQD as the carbon material and the flower ball-like His-GQD/LDH is synthesized via microwave method. Moreover, the asymmetric supercapacitor is assembled using His-GQD/LDH, active carbon, and 6 M KOH as positive electrode, negative electrode and electrolyte, respectively.

A flower ball-like histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dots/Ni-Co LDH (His-GQD/LDH) composite is synthesized via microwave method. The GQDs are uniformly interspersed on LDH surface and the radius of flower balls is approximately 200 nm. The synergistic effect of His-GQD and LDH can signally increase the specific surface areas and conductivity of the composite, thus endowing the composite high specific capacitance (1526 F g−1) and an admirable cycling stability (82.36% capacitance retention). Moreover, we have manufactured a supercapacitor employing His-GQD/LDH and active carbon (AC) as positive/negative electrodes. The device exhibits the maximum energy of 48.89 W h kg−1 at 0.80 kW kg−1, as well as prominent cycling stability (91.13% capacitance retention). This work provides a practicle experimental method of synthesizing hybridizing histidine-functionalized carbon derivatives with LDH by microwave synthesis. Meanwhile, we are optimistic to believe that the electrode material can be extensively applied for supercapacitor because of its splendid electrochemical properties and facile synthesis.

ce:section.ce:para: As society develops, energy and pollution problems become more and more serious, people are beginning to use clean energy to obtain electricity. Among variety of energy storage and conversion devices, supercapacitors possess several merits such as fast charging and discharging, high power delivery, and excellent cycling lifespan [1,2]. However, supercapacitors also have the obvious disadvantage of having a lower energy density than batteries, which hinders their further application. At present, the electrode materials have been the key of energy storage for supercapacitors [3,4]. Therefore, researchers are keen to find new electrode materials to continuously improve the performance of supercapacitors.

LDH, a kind of anionic clay, has attracted extensive attention owing to its intriguing properties, such as abundant feeding source, low toxicity, high theoretical capacitance and tunable composition [5,6]. The material can deliver relatively high capacitance but is limited by poor stability because of structural degradation of the electrode during the redox process. However, the problem can be solved by incorporating carbon materials with LDH. Yang et al. reported NiCoAl-LDH grown on graphite paper, which specific capacitance could reach up to 1297 F g−1 [7]. Yu et al. synthesized NiCoAl-LDH-carbon nanohybrids with a specific capacitance of 1188 F g−1 [8]. Wang et al. obtained the RGO/LDH nanocomposite with a specific capacitance of 1055 F g−1 [9].

GQDs are a new type of carbon nanomaterials with tiny size, which not only have various properties of graphene, such as high electron and hole mobility, high electron emission, fast electron transport speed, and large theoretical surface area, but also have good biocompatibility, photoluminescent properties, chemical inertness, and the particularly remarkable quantum confinement effect and boundary effect [10,11]. These features make GQDs popular with researchers in recent years. Many investigations have demonstrated that the introduction of hetero atoms and functional groups may drastically alter the electronic characteristics of GQD, thus leading to unusual properties and related applications. Herein, His-GQD were prepared by pyrolysis using citric acid and histidine as mixed carbon sources to generate graphene quantum dots with special functional groups. His-GQD not only carry abundant groups which can effectively link LDH, but also possess favorable conductivity by N-doping and thus make electron transportion more rapidly [12,13]. Notably, the histidine as nitrogen source is environmentally friendly.

Microwave irradiation is often applied for the rapid synthesis of inorganic solids and organic synthetic reactions. Compared with hydrothermal method, microwave synthesis has the advantages of energy saving, high efficiency, short reaction time and uniform heating [14]. In this work, flower ball-like His-GQD/LDH with super performance of superior specific capacitance of 1526 F g−1 was synthesized via microwave method. Furthermore, we employed His-GQD/LDH and AC as positive and negative electrodes respectively to fabricate an asymmetric supercapacitor, which exhibited 48.89 W h kg−1 at 0.80 kW kg−1 and remarkable cycling stability of 91.13% capacitance retention.

5.2535 g citric acid and 3.4875 g histidine were dissolved in 40 mL of deionized water, then the solution was transferred to a water-bath stirring pot and continuously stirred for 1 h at 80 °C. After the homogeneous solution was formed, the water was evaporated to obtain colloidal substance, which was transferred to the oven and kept at 200 °C for 3 h to obtain crude His-GQD. We dissolved the crude His-GQD into deionized water and 3000 Da dialysis bag was used to remove unreacted raw materials (change water every 4/6 h). Finally, His-GQD sample was obtained by freeze drying.

40 mg His-GQD, 0.2913 g cobalt nitrate hexahydrate, 0.2908 g nickel nitrate hexahydrate and 0.4805 g urea were dissolved in 20 mL of 30% ethylene glycol solution to generate mixed solution, then continually stirred the mixture for 24 h to form a homogeneous solution. Subsequently, the solution was transferred to a microwave reactor for 10 min at 190 °C. Finally, His-GQD/LDH sample was obtained after washing, filtration and drying.

The active material, acetylene black, and appropriate amount of ethanol (as solvent) were mixed in a mortar and grounded continuously for 40 min. Then a little PTFE was drip-fed and the mixture was regrounded to form a paste (the mass ratio of the above three solutes were 8:1:1). The paste was spread on nickel foam and dried to obtain electrodes. For comparison, control samples were obtained via the same condition. The loading masses of Ni(OH)2, Co(OH)2, LDH and His-GQD/LDH were 1.21, 0.98, 1.25, and 1.17 mg cm−2.

The morphology and structure were investigated by SU3900 and HT7820 scanning/transmission electron microscope (SEM/TEM). The curves of nitrogen adsorption-desorption, Raman, XRD and FT-IR were recorded by ASAP 2020, XperRam S, ARL EQUNINX 3500 and Nicolet 8700 equipments. Furthermore, the element valence analysis was obtained via PHI 5000 VersaProbe II X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Finally, the Zennium E electrochemical workstation was carried out to measure the electrochemical properties (CV, GCD, EIS) of electrodes and supercapacitor. The prepared electrode, Pt plate, Hg/HgO electrode, and 6 M KOH were used as working, counter, reference electrodes and electrolyte.

The supercapacitor was fabricated with His-GQD/LDH and AC as the positive and negative electrodes respectively, 6 M KOH solution as electrolyte. The preparation process of AC electrode was similar to His-GQD/LDH electrode. In order to obtain the specific capacitance, energy and power density of the device, the following formulas are used for calculation [15–17]:
(1)
C = I × t / ( Δ V × m )
(2)
E = 0.5 ×
C s
× Δ
V 2
(3)
P = E × 3600 / Δ t

The load mass of AC electrode is given by charge balance theory, and the corresponding formulas are as follows [18,19]:
(4)
q = C × Δ V × m
(5)
m +
/
m -
=
C -
× Δ
V -
/
C +
× Δ
V +

Here C, Cs, represent the capacitance of electrodes and supercapacitor. I, t, V indicate current density, discharge time and voltage range. P and E are the energy and power density. q and m+/
− represent charge quantity and the loading of positive/negative electrodes. Moreover, the optimal mass ratio of m(His-GQD/LDH)/m(AC) is 0.32 in this supercapacitor.

The illustration of flower ball-like His-GQD/LDH formation is given in Fig. S1. At first, OH− comes from the reaction of NO3 − with C2H6O2. Meanwhile, the urea decomposition at high temperature/pressure can also generate OH− (Eqs. (6)–(10)) [20–22]:
(6)
4
C 2
H 6
O 2
+ 2 N
O 3 -
→ 4
C 2
H 2
O 2
+ 2 N
H 3
+ 2 O
H -
+ 4
H 2
O
(7)
N
H 2
C O N
H 2
→ N
H 4 +
+ C N
O -
(8)
CN
O -
+ 3
H 2
O → H C
O 3 -
+ N
H 4 +
+ O
H -
(9)
N
H 2
C O N
H 2
+
H 2
O → 2 N
H 3
+ C
O 2
(10)
N
H 3
+
H 2
O → N
H 4 +
+ O
H -
Subsequently, Ni2+ and Co2+ react with OH− in the solution at a microwave reactor.

The XRD patterns in Fig. 1 show the diffraction peaks of His-GQD/LDH and control samples. The diffraction peaks in XRD curve correspond to the (0 0 3), (0 0 6), (1 0 1), (0 1 2) and (1 1 0) planes of α-phase Ni-Co LDH (Fig. 1a). The interlayer spacing is 7.7 Å and calculated by Bragg equation, which is in line with the reported literatures [23,24]. In Fig. 1b, the peak intensity of Ni(OH)2 is higher than LDH, and the peak of (1 1 0) plane is shifted to the left after Co ions introduction. This can be attributed that Co doping changes the d-spacing [25]. In addition, His-GQD has one peak at approximately 25°. However, we cannot clearly observe this peak in His-GQD/LDH sample because the size and content of His-GQD are too small and too low. Therefore, the crystallization degree of His-GQD is relatively lower. Moreover, there is a His-GQD/LDH diffraction peak at around 25°, so we think it is also possible that the diffraction peak of His-GQD/LDH covers the diffraction peak of His-GQD. Furthermore, FT-IR was used to obtain better information about the connection between LDH with the His-GQD (Fig. S2). The intense band at 3639 cm−1 relates to the stretching vibration of non-hydrogen bonded hydroxyl groups, which affirms the structure of Ni, Co hydroxide phase. The large band centered at 3470 cm−1 is assigned to the OH stretching vibration, confirming the asprepared sample possesses rich hydrophilic hydroxyl groups. The two weak bands at 2923 and 2860 cm−1 are characteristics of the CH vibration mode of CH2. The peak at 1697 cm−1 can be assigned to the CO stretch vibration [26]. The less intense band at 1623 cm−1 is ascribed to the bending mode of water molecules in the interlayer. The bands at 1541, 1366 and 1063 cm−1 appear corresponding to the bending vibration of CNH, NO3 − (residue from Ni or Co nitrate) and CN. At 840 and 630 cm−1, these peaks are attributed to the characteristic vibrations of Ni/CoO, Ni/CoONi/Co and ONi/CoO stretching [27].

The chemical composition of His-GQD/LDH sample was analyzed by XPS. The survey spectrum exhibits the existing of C, N, Co, and Ni elements (Fig. S3). Among them, C and N elements are from the histidine. The C 1s spectrum (Fig. 2 a) can be divided into several distinguishable peaks at 284.5, 285.3, 287.8 and 289.3 eV (CC, CO, CO and OCO) [28]. The Co 2p spectrum is shown in Fig. 2b. Those peaks can be assigned as two major peaks at 797.1 and 781.2 eV, corresponding to the Co 2p1/2 and Co 2p3/2. Furthermore, the Co 2p also has two satellite peaks at 803.1 and 786.3 eV [24]. The N 1s spectrum is shown in Fig. 2c, which can be fitted into the peaks at 398.6, 399.8 and 401.0 eV (Pyridinic, Pyrrolic and Graphitic N) [29]. The Ni 2p spectrum (Fig. 2d) exhibits double major (satellite) peaks at 855.9 and 873.5 (861.8 and 879.8) eV corresponding to the Ni 2p3/2 and Ni 2p1/2 [30]. The above results confirm that His-GQD has successfully combined with LDH.

The existing of His-GQD in the samples was further confirmed via Raman spectra (Fig. S4). The D and G peaks of Raman spectra present the defects in the atomic lattices and telescopic vibration of C sp2. Meanwhile, The D/G ratio is also an important indicator to characterize the degree of sample disorder [31]. We can clearly observe that the D and G peaks of His-GQD are nearly 1343 and 1586 cm−1. As well as, the spectrum of His-GQD/LDH is similar to His-GQD. Moreover, the ID/IG of His-GQD/LDH is higher than His-GQD, suggesting that His-GQD is reduced during microwave reaction [32].

The pore structures of the samples were explored by BET. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the BET surface areas of His-GQD/LDH, LDH, Ni(OH)2 and Co(OH)2 are 60.9860, 33.3044, 22.0518, and 28.7284 m2 g−1. In addition, the curve of His-GQD/LDH presents type IV N2 isotherms because it possesses hysteresis loop at 0.4–1.0 P/P0 [33]. The BHJ pore size distributions of all samples are exhibited in Fig. 3b. In comparison with Ni(OH)2 and Co(OH)2 (nearly 50 nm), the pore size distributions of His-GQD/LDH and LDH appear at approximately 24 nm, indicating that they possess more larger surface areas. The flower ball-like structure of His-GQD/LDH not only provides high surface areas to accelerate reaction, but also facilitates charge transfer, which improves the electrochemical properties of the material.

The morphologies of all samples are presented in Fig. 4 . The HRSEM (Fig. 4a and c) present disorderly stack sheet-like structure of Ni(OH)2 and Co(OH)2. Furthermore, the large amounts agglomeration of nanosheets can be observed from Fig. 4b and d, whose sizes are approximately 600 nm. However, unlike Ni(OH)2 and Co(OH)2, LDH possesses a flower ball-like structure and the radius is nearly 500 nm (Fig. 4e and f). Similar to LDH, His-GQD/LDH has the same flower spherical structure with the radius of about 200 nm (Fig. 4g and h). Furthermore, the His-GQD particles are uniformly dispersed in LDH petals. Due to this structure, the agglomeration of the sample is greatly reduced, which is proved by the perfect flower ball structure from Fig. 4h.

More information of His-GQD/LDH was investigated by the TEM. The flower ball-like structure of His-GQD/LDH is observed from Fig. 5 a (for clarity, the SEM and TEM images of His-GQD are exhibited in Fig. S5), and the balls consist of Ni(OH)2 and Co(OH)2 nanosheet petals (Fig. 5b). The diffraction rings (inset in Fig. 5c) can be observed from SAED pattern obviously, indicating that the (0 0 3), (1 0 1) and (1 1 0) planes of LDH. The HRTEM image of Fig. 5c reveals that the fringe spacings of 0.267 and 0.154 nm, corresponding to the (1 0 1) and (1 1 0) lattice planes of LDH. Finally, in Fig. 5d, the EDS elemental mapping demonstrates that LDH and His-GQD are composited successfully (EDS spectrum and element concentration are given in Fig. S6).

The electrochemical properties of all electrodes were initially evaluated by CV. The CV behaviors of His-GQD/LDH, LDH, Ni(OH)2 and Co(OH)2 are presented in Fig. 6 a. The redox peaks can be observed in all samples, and those behaviors derive from the transformation of Ni/Co ions valence states. As shown in the Eqs. (11)–(13) [30,34,35]:
(11)
Ni
( O H )
2
+ O
H -
⇔ N i O O H +
H 2
O +
e -
(12)
Co
( O H )
2
+ O
H -
⇔ C o O O H +
H 2
O +
e -
(13)
CoOOH + O
H -
⇔ C o
O 2
+
H 2
O +
e -

The CV loop area of the His-GQD/LDH electrode is larger than those of other electrodes, suggesting that it has the highest specific capacitance. We also have investigated the CV behaviors of His-GQD/LDH electrode at 5–40 mV s−1 (Fig. 6b). When the scan rate increases to 40 mV s−1, the redox peak has a little excursion because it is affected by polarization. However, the shape of CV curve is still similar to that of 5 mV s−1. This result demonstrates that the electrode possesses outstanding reversibility [15,36]. Furthermore, the reaction kinetics of the electrodes can also be studied from the CV curves. The specific relationship is as Eqs. (14)–(16):
(14)
i = a
v b
(15)
log i = b log v + log a
(16)
i
( V )
/
v
1
/
2
=
k 1
v
1
/
2
+
k 2
where i, v, a, b indicate the current, scanning rate, and the adjustable parameters, respectively. To obtain the value of b, the logarithm of both sides of Eq. (14) is taken, and subsequently linear fitting of log i and log v is done. Meanwhile, the value of b also indicates that the electrodes possess battery or pseudo-capacitor properties. The V, k1, k2 present the specified voltage and the adjustable parameters, respectively. In the same way, the value of k1 can be obtained by linear fitting i(V)/v 1/2 and v 1/2 in formula (16) under the specified voltage. Finally, k1 v is the contribution of pseudocapacitor to the current at each specific voltage.

The b value of His-GQD/LDH is found to be higher than that of LDH and Ni(OH)2 (0.63 > 0.59 and 0.56), indicating its faster reaction kinetics (Fig. 6c). The percentage of diffusion/capacitance control is shown in Fig. 6d. As expected, in low scanning rates, the diffusion control is dominant. Upon increasing the scanning rate, the contribution of diffusion control begins to decrease (Contribution fitting CV curves are shown in Fig. S7).

The GCD curves of all electrodes are exhibited and compared in Fig. 7 a. The t of His-GQD/LDH electrode is longer than those of other electrodes, indicating that it has the highest capacitance. This is also in line with CV analysis. In Fig. 7b, the relationship of C and I is investigated, and I gradually increasing results in C decreasing. However, compared with other electrodes (Fig. S8), the capacitance of His-GQD/LDH electrode decreases from 1526 F g−1 to 1372 F g−1, and corresponding a rate capability of 89.19%. The value is higher than those of LDH, Ni(OH)2 and Co(OH)2 (86.79%, 80.73% and 87.62%, respectively. As shown in Fig. 7c). This result is ascribed that the Co introduction reduces the band gap of His-GQD/LDH, thus facilitating the charge transfer. Because the smaller the band gap is, the easier the electron transition from valence band to conduction band. At the same time, in the electrochemical reaction, the CoOOH generated by Co(OH)2 (as described in Eqs. (12)) has higher conductivity and is more beneficial to improve the rate performance of His-GQD/LDH [24,37].

The electrodes were measured at 10 A g−1 to analyse the cycling stability (Fig. 7d). After 2000 cycles, His-GQD/LDH, LDH, Ni(OH)2 and Co(OH)2 electrodes reduce from 1372, 880, 620 and 252 F g−1 to 1130, 720, 440 and 180 F g−1, corresponding cycling stabilities are 82.36%, 81.82%, 70.97% and 71.43%, respectively. The splendid electrochemical properties derive from the special structure of His-GQD/LDH and the introduction of His-GQD. Above all, the unique structure of His-GQD/LDH is different from those of Ni(OH)2 and Co(OH)2, which can expand the surface areas and facilitate the ion transfer to reactivity with active material, thus increasing the electrochemical properties of the electrode. In addition, the introduction of His-GQD also enhances the electrode electrical conductivity, and restrains the volume expansion of Ni and Co during charging-discharging process. Hence, it signally enhances the cyclic stability of the electrode, which also displays super performances compared to some previous reports (the correlation data exhibit in Table S1, the XRD patterns and SEM, TEM images of all electrode materials after long-time cycling are provided in Fig. S9, 10 and 11).

The electrochemical diffusion process of electrodes were studied by EIS. As shown in Fig. 8 , all curves consist of a straight line and a semicircle, which indicate the low and high frequency regions [38–41]. In addition, the equivalent circuit is also given in inset. The Rs and Rct of His-GQD/LDH, LDH, Ni(OH)2 and Co(OH)2 electrodes are 0.37, 0.61, 0.75, 0.40 Ω and 0.44, 0.93, 0.21 Ω, 0.28 Ω. These data demonstrate that His-GQD/LDH electrode has relatively smaller Rs and Rct. Meanwhile, the inclination of the His-GQD/LDH line is also larger, indicating that it has the smaller Warburg impedance. Considering comprehensively, the His-GQD/LDH electrode has the best combination of Rs, Rct and Warburg impedance. The ideal impedance of this electrode is ascribed that the wonderful flower ball-like structure increases the ion diffusion path, and the uniform distribution of His-GQD in the petals can also effectively improve the conductivity of the electrode. Consequently, the electrochemical impedance of His-GQD/LDH electrode is reduced and its performances are enhanced. Furthermore, after 2000 times cycle, the EIS spectra are presented in Fig. S12.

An asymmetric supercapacitor was fabricated to prove that the His-GQD/LDH electrode possessed practical value. The schematic diagrams are presented in Fig. 9 a and b. Based on the previously measured CV of His-GQD/LDH and AC (its CV and GCD curves are exhibited in Figs. S13 and S8), we preliminarily determine that the voltage range of the asymmetric supercapacitor is 0–1.6 V, as shown in Fig. 9c [42,43]. Fig. 9d records CV curves of the device at different potential windows at 50 mV s−1. As expected, CV curves maintain a similar trend, which suggests prominent capacitive behavior of the device. Furthermore, the potential window can even exceed the initial estimate of 1.6 V and reach 1.8 V. However, in order to ensure the smooth running of the device, the voltage range of the device is finally determined to be 0–1.6 V. The CV curves at different scan rates are given in Fig. 10 a. The shapes of the curves are comparable and look like a leaf, which can be attributed to the fusion of electric double layer and faradaic capacitance. The GCD curves at 1–10 A g−1 are exhibited in Fig. 10b. The Cs of device are 138, 127, 92, 88, 85 and 80 F g−1 by calculating from GCD curves. Ragone plot of device is displayed in Fig. 10c. Remarkably, the device possesses energy density of 48.89 Wh kg−1 at 0.80 kW kg−1. Even if the power density increases to 8 kW kg−1, the device still maintains 28.53 Wh kg−1. Here, the supercapacitor delivers higher energy and power density than those in previous reports (the correlation data exhibit in Table S2) [44–48]. The cycling stability of device, as well as the inset photo of initial and terminal GCD curves are presented in Fig. 10d. After 6000 cycles, the device still possesses 91.13% capacitance retention, which indicates its outstanding cycling performance. Finally, several LED are lighted by this supercapacitor successfully (inset image of Fig. 10c), Which demonstrates the practicability and potential value of His-GQD/LDH electrode.

The His-GQD/LDH electrode was obtained via microwave synthesis method. It exhibits 1526 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 and the rate capability is nearly 89.19%. Its exceptional performances come from the His-GQD introduction to the flower ball-like structural LDH, the unique combination not only facilitates electrolyte ions reacting with active material, but also reduces the impedance and improves the cyclic stability. Furthermore, we also manufactured the supercapacitor with His-GQD/LDH and AC electrodes as positive and negative pole. It delivers maximum energy density of 48.89 Wh kg−1 at 0.80 kW kg−1, and after 6000 cycles, the capacitance retains approximately 91.13%. Moreover, several LED are lighted by this supercapacitor successfully, which suggests the practicability of His-GQD/LDH electrode. In the end, owing to the electrode possesses outstanding advantages, such as high performances, environmental friendliness, convenient synthesis process, inexpensive raw materials. Therefore, we optimistically believe that the electrode will be widely used for supercapacitor.

Hengrui Qiu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Xuejiao Sun: Investigation, Data curation. Shengli An: Resources, Supervision. Dawei Lan: Data curation. Jinlong Cui: Validation. Yongqiang Zhang: Project administration, Funding acquisition. Wenxiu He: Project administration, Funding acquisition.
This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China and Natural Science Foundation of Inner Mongolia (No.21766024, 51864039 and 2019MS02022).
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2020.02.018.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Flexible asymmetric supercapacitors based on CuO@MnO2-rGO and MoS2-rGO with ultrahigh energy density
